given_idea: Using a pseudo-parallel corpus generated by an unsupervised machine translation model facilitates the structural similarity of the two embedding spaces and improves the quality of cross-lingual word embeddings in the unsupervised mapping method.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: A fully unsupervised framework for learning multilingual word embeddings will substantially outperform previous approaches in multilingual word translation and cross-lingual word similarity.

Unsupervised bilingual word embeddings jointly trained on synthetic parallel data significantly outperform unsupervised mapped bilingual word embeddings in several cross-lingual natural language processing tasks.
An unsupervised learning approach will optimize transformation functions between monolingual word embedding spaces without requiring cross-lingual labeled data.
Cross-lingual sentence embeddings will outperform context-independent word mapping in sentence translation retrieval.
Training unsupervised neural machine translation with unsupervised bilingual word embedding agreement will significantly improve its performance compared to conventional methods.
The performance of unsupervised bilingual word embedding and cross-lingual masked language model has a significant influence on the performance of unsupervised neural machine translation.
Aligning monolingual word embedding spaces in an unsupervised way can build a bilingual dictionary between two languages without using any parallel corpora.
Neighborhood sensitive maps for multilingual word embeddings (NORMA) outperforms current state-of-the-art methods for word translation between distant languages.
Using word embeddings for word alignment will outperform previous word embeddings-based methods in segment-level machine translation evaluation.
An unsupervised system for parallel corpus filtering will improve the quality of machine translation systems for low-resource languages.

The proposed unsupervised multilingual embedding method performs well under resource-poor scenarios, significantly outperforms existing unsupervised and supervised methods trained with bilingual pairs of words.
Incorporating word and subword units in unsupervised machine translation will improve the fluency and consistency of translations.
Resource-lean cross-lingual embedding models may outperform resource-intensive models using full-blown machine translation in some settings.

High quality crosslingual word embeddings can be built for many languages in a unified vector space, enabling high performance on bilingual lexicon induction, monolingual similarity, and crosslingual document classification tasks.
The structure-aware output layer will improve translation quality by capturing the semantic structure of the output space of words within a joint input-output embedding.
Iterative Normalization consistently improves word translation accuracy of three cross-lingual word embedding methods.
The proposed formulation ensures composable mappings, leading to better alignments of multilingual word embeddings.

Fully unsupervised cross-lingual word embedding methods fail to induce meaningful cross-lingual word embeddings for a large number of language pairs and never surpass the performance of weakly supervised methods.
The proposed loss function will improve neural machine translation performance by incorporating word similarity in the embedding space.
Clustering languages using language embedding improves translation accuracy for almost all languages over baseline methods.
The reproduction of the robust self-learning method for fully unsupervised cross-lingual mappings of word embeddings is feasible with some minor assumptions.
Learning unsupervised word mapping by directly maximizing the mean discrepancy between the distribution of transferred embedding and target embedding will outperform competitive baselines.

Neural machine translation context vectors serve as an effective interlingua representation for parallel sentence identification, achieving an F1 score of 98.9% when combined with similarity measures.

An unsupervised method that automatically builds the monolingual parallel corpus for text simplification using sentence similarity based on word embeddings will outperform existing methods that require human annotation.
Cross-language aligned word embeddings as the sole source of semantic information mirror human error annotation in neural machine translation.
Cross-lingual semantic similarity between five European languages is measurable using unsupervised word embeddings and various distance measures.
Modularity can serve as an intrinsic validation metric to improve unsupervised cross-lingual word embeddings, particularly on distant language pairs in low-resource settings.
Embeddings from translation models outperform those learned by monolingual models at tasks that require knowledge of both conceptual similarity and lexical-syntactic role.
The shared embedding architecture will improve cross-lingual information retrieval by effectively handling polysemy and synonymy without explicit translation.

The robust self-learning method for fully unsupervised cross-lingual mappings of word embeddings succeeds in all tested scenarios and obtains the best published results in standard datasets, even surpassing previous supervised systems.
Bilingual word embeddings significantly out-perform baselines in word semantic similarity.
Extracting parallel sentences from comparable corpora significantly improves the performance of statistical machine translation systems.
Bilingual embeddings learned using the BilBOWA model outperform state-of-the-art methods on a cross-lingual document classification task as well as a lexical translation task on WMT11 data.
Character-based embeddings in neural machine translation will improve results compared to standard lookup-based word representations.
The discriminative word alignment model integrated into a Transformer-based machine translation model will achieve major improvements over unsupervised baselines in cross-lingual word alignment.

Unsupervised neural machine translation (UNMT) initialized by synthetic bilingual data generated by unsupervised statistical machine translation (USMT) achieves a new state-of-the-art for unsupervised machine translation on the WMT16 German--English news translation task.
Bilingual sentence embeddings can be used to reconstruct the United Nations Parallel Corpus at the sentence-level with a precision of 48.9% for English-French and 54.9% for English-Spanish.
Minimizing earth mover's distance between word embedding spaces will successfully establish cross-lingual connections for unsupervised bilingual lexicon induction.
Adding additional sources of information beyond the traditional signal of bilingual sentence-aligned corpora may substantially improve cross-lingual word embeddings.

The approach that expresses the two monolingual embedding spaces as probability densities defined by a Gaussian mixture model will achieve competitive or superior performance compared to state-of-the-art published results in bilingual lexicon induction and cross-lingual word similarity.
The Parallel Meaning Bank will provide a multilingual corpus of translations annotated with compositional meaning representations.
The proposed method obtains state of the art results on the task of unsupervised word translation while requiring less computational resources than competing approaches.
Using sentence embedding similarity to select sentences close to in-domain data will substantially improve Neural Machine Translation performance.
Shared-private bilingual word embeddings will provide a significant performance boost over strong baselines with dramatically fewer model parameters in neural machine translation.
The proposed method for margin-based parallel corpus mining with multilingual sentence embeddings outperforms existing methods, achieving significant improvements in F1 and precision metrics.
Regressing word embeddings (ReWE) improves the generalization of neural machine translation systems in low-resource settings.
Both characters and embeddings achieve good performance in scoring the outputs of machine translation systems.
Pre-trained word embeddings can be surprisingly effective in improving performance for Neural Machine Translation tasks, providing gains of up to 20 BLEU points in favorable settings.
Deep canonical correlation analysis will improve the quality of word embeddings when incorporating multilingual context compared to monolingual embeddings and linear canonical correlation analysis.
A distribution-based model will effectively learn bilingual word embeddings from monolingual data without requiring parallel data or seed lexicon.

given_idea: The lack of semantic alignment between translations and the inability to penalize low-quality literal translations hinders cross-lingual encoders' ability to evaluate machine translation quality.
original: Cross-lingual encoders perform poorly as semantic encoders for reference-free machine translation evaluation due to a semantic mismatch between representations of mutual translations and the inability to punish low-quality literal translations.

top_k.sentence: Cross-lingual encoders perform poorly as semantic encoders for reference-free machine translation evaluation due to a semantic mismatch between representations of mutual translations and the inability to punish low-quality literal translations.

The performance of evaluation metrics varies significantly across different levels of Machine Translation quality, with metrics performing poorly when faced with low-quality translations and not capturing nuanced quality distinctions.
Translationese negatively impacts the accuracy of machine translation evaluations.

This paper attempts to bridge the gap by introducing a metric for measuring literality of translations and assesses the effort that is observed when translators produce translations which deviate from the introduced literality definition.
Document-level evaluation is necessary for accurately assessing the quality of machine translation as errors that are hard to spot at the sentence-level become decisive.
The use of translationese in test sets results in inflated human evaluation scores for machine translation systems.
Human–machine parity in language translation is influenced by evaluation design weaknesses, the choice of raters, the availability of linguistic context, and the creation of reference translations.
Different alignment models will impact the translation quality of a statistical machine translation system.
Machine Translation will need to judge output based on notions of fluency and adequacy with reference to the source text.
Annotators are strongly biased by the reference translation provided, and this can have a negative impact on the assessment of machine translation quality.
Semantic parsing can be approached as a machine translation task, and machine translation methods can serve as an informative baseline in semantic parsing evaluations.
The WMT evaluation campaign has demonstrated that human judgement should be the ultimate arbiter of machine translation quality.
The machine translation system must provide facilities for the user to check the quality of the translation without knowing the foreign language.
Round-trip translation is a poor predictor of the overall quality of machine translation systems and the translatability of a given text.
Evaluating machine translation performance for six European language pairs will yield insights into the effectiveness of automatic and manual evaluation methods.
The ranking of machine translation systems strongly correlates with human judgments of translation quality.
The proposed quantitative quality measure will evaluate the performance of machine translation systems by comparing raw translation output with the final revised version.
Employing semantic embeddings to round-trip translation-based quality estimation metric will achieve higher correlations with human judgments compared to existing metrics.
One out of four test items in German-to-English machine translation systems is translated incorrectly on average.
The longest common subsequence and skip-bigram statistics methods correlate with human judgments in terms of adequacy and fluency for machine translation quality evaluation.
The inclusion of both syntactic and semantic information can improve translation quality.
The task of predicting the quality of the output of machine translation systems can be effectively evaluated at three granularity levels: word, sentence, and document.
Automatic metrics correlate with human judgments of translation quality.
Detecting content-heavy sentences in cross-lingual context will improve the understandability of translations for English speakers.
Bridging the word order gap leads to significant improvement in translation quality in extremely low-resource scenarios.
Encoder-free models have different effects on alignment quality for German → English and Chinese → English.
Character n-gram F-score (CHRF2) is the most promising version for evaluating machine translation outputs.
Discourse factors significantly impact the quality of machine translation.
An automatic evaluation metric based on rank correlation coefficients modified with precision will outperform conventional metrics for evaluating translation quality between distant language pairs.
Subjective human judgements can be effectively replaced with an automatic annotation procedure in Machine Translation Quality Estimation.
Human parity has not been achieved in machine translation when considering only original source text.
Cross-language aligned word embeddings as the sole source of semantic information mirror human error annotation in neural machine translation.
It is possible to improve the machine translation quality of the majority of the systems participating in the WMT shared task.
An improved Bleu score is neither necessary nor sufficient for achieving an actual improvement in translation quality.
Tuning a machine translation system against a semantic frame based objective function produces more robustly adequate translations than tuning against BLEU or TER.
Automatic metrics correlate with human judgments of translation quality for evaluation metrics.
The NIST metric is more sensitive than BLEU or F-score in evaluating machine translation.
Marmot is a new toolkit for quality estimation of machine translation output.

Cross-mutual information (XMI) allows for a better evaluation of the difficulty of translating text into the target language while controlling for the difficulty of the target-side generation component independent of the translation task.
Current Machine Translation systems fail to render the lexical diversity of human-generated or translated text.
Optimizing a fixed system to different metrics will result in perceptibly different translation quality.
Error analysis should be an integral part of machine translation development to provide qualitative insights not obtained by standard evaluation methods.
Modeling translationese and original text as separate languages in a multilingual model can improve the naturalness of machine translation outputs.
The methodology improves correlation with all modern evaluation metrics for machine translation.
IBM1 scores are competitive with classic evaluation metrics for machine translation quality.
An automatic classification of translation processes at subsentential level can distinguish non-literal translation from literal translation with an accuracy of 87.09%.
The quantity of monolingual corpus significantly affects the accuracy of statistical machine translation from English to Indonesian.
Translation process can introduce subtle artifacts that have a notable impact in existing cross-lingual models.
Character n-gram F-score for automatic evaluation of machine translation output will show high segment-level correlations, outperforming existing metrics on the WMT14 shared evaluation task.
Current state-of-the-art methods perform rather poorly on the FLORES evaluation datasets for low-resource machine translation.

given_idea: TransESC will generate more smooth and effective supportive responses compared to previous methods in Emotion Support Conversation.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: Recognizing the emotion cause improves empathetic response generation in conversations.
The Emotional Support Conversation (ESC) task and the ESC Framework will enhance the ability of dialog systems to provide effective emotional support.
Emotional Chatting Machine (ECM) can generate appropriate responses not only in content but also in emotion.
Employing a multi-modal approach will improve emotion recognition accuracy in conversations.
Positive emotion elicitation in chat-based dialogue systems will result in dialogue responses that are more natural and elicit a more positive emotional response.
Incorporating emotional tags in the conversation generation model will enable the chatbot to generate responses that are appropriate in both content and emotional context.
Incorporating emotional context into the dialogue response generation process will significantly improve the naturalness of responses and elicit more positive emotions from users.
ChatGPT exhibits promising results in generating emotional responses, although its performance on emotional dialogue understanding may still lag behind that of supervised models.
HiTrans is a transformer-based context- and speaker-sensitive model for emotion detection in conversations that outperforms previous state-of-the-art models.
The proposed empathetic chatbot will effectively utilize causal emotion information to improve empathetic responding compared to several state-of-the-art methods.
Emotion recognition in conversation (ERC) is becoming increasingly popular as a new research frontier in natural language processing due to its ability to mine opinions from conversational data.
Feedback-Aware Double Controlling Network (FADO) will achieve state-of-the-art performance in terms of both strategy selection and response generation for Emotional Support Conversation.

A heterogeneous graph-based model for emotional conversation generation will effectively perceive emotions from multi-source knowledge and generate satisfactory responses, significantly outperforming previous state-of-the-art models.
The two-stage conversational agent for emotional dialogue generation will outperform comparison models in emotion generation while maintaining semantic performance.
The Multimodal EmotionLines Dataset (MELD) will enhance emotion recognition in conversations by providing a large-scale multimodal multi-party emotional conversational database.
The emotional dialogue system (EmoDS) can generate meaningful responses with a coherent structure while expressing the desired emotion explicitly or implicitly within a unified framework.
User emotion look-ahead is key to modeling and generating empathetic dialogue responses.
The MSP-Conversation corpus contains interactions annotated with time-continuous emotional traces for arousal, valence, and dominance.
CoMPM significantly improves the performance of the context model for Emotion Recognition in Conversation.
Emotion Eliciting Machine (EEM) will outperform existing models in generating responses with positive emotion elicitation.
Equipping the dialog system with personality will enable it to automatically select emotions in responses by simulating the emotion transition of humans in conversation.
The positive-emotion-guided dialog generation model will effectively elicit positive emotions from users and ensure smooth emotional transitions in multi-turn dialogs.
Knowledge-Enriched Transformer (KET) outperforms state-of-the-art models on most tested datasets in F1 score for emotion detection in textual conversations.
DialogXL will outperform existing models for emotion recognition in conversation across multiple benchmarks.
An affect-driven dialog system generates emotional responses in a controlled manner using a continuous representation of emotions.
EmpTransfo utilizes the history of emotions and other metadata to improve the quality of generated conversations by the dialog system.
CAiRE achieves state-of-the-art performance on dialogue emotion detection and empathetic response generation.
Combining rationality and emotion into conversational agents can improve response quality.
A Hierarchical Transformer with Speaker Modeling will achieve better performance in Emotion Recognition in Conversation compared to existing methods.
The emotion cause transition graph will produce more empathetic, coherent, informative, and specific responses than existing models.
An iterative emotion interaction network will effectively retain the performance advantages of explicit modeling in emotion recognition in conversations.
The large-scale balanced Multimodal Multi-label Emotion, Intensity, and Sentiment Dialogue dataset (MEISD) will improve emotion recognition and sentiment analysis in conversations.
MMGCN will outperform other state-of-the-art methods in emotion recognition in conversation by effectively utilizing multimodal dependencies and speaker information.
Topic-Driven Knowledge-Aware Transformer will outperform existing state-of-the-art approaches in dialogue emotion detection.
The Dialogue Emotion Recognition Challenge, EmotionX, will provide a dataset and evaluation results that facilitate the recognition of emotions in dialogues.
Contextual dialogue can improve emotion detection in text.
A large-scale silver dataset consisting of 1M dialogues annotated with 32 fine-grained emotions will enhance the development of emotion-aware chatbots.
The proposed model obtains significantly more favorable results than the state-of-the-art model in dialogue emotion recognition.
The proposed unified end-to-end neural architecture will generate more intelligent responses with appropriately expressed emotions compared to existing methods.
HEAL will generate more diverse, empathetic, and reliable responses compared to existing end-to-end empathetic conversational agents.
Dialogue Graph Convolutional Network will outperform current state of the art methods for emotion recognition in conversation.

Conversational agents' response styles under varying abuse types influence user emotions, with the empathy response style leading to less anger and more guilt compared to avoidance and counterattacking styles.
Supervised Prototypical Contrastive Learning (SPCL) loss will improve emotion recognition in conversation by addressing the imbalanced classification problem through contrastive learning.
Conditional variational autoencoders will successfully generate high-quality abstractive conversation responses in accordance with designated emotions.

The conversational agent's response style has a significant effect on user emotions, with participants feeling less angry and more guilty when interacting with the empathetic agent compared to the other agents.
Pre-training on unsupervised data achieves significant improvement of performance on the Emotion Recognition in Conversations datasets, particularly on the minority emotion classes.
The model that adds the emotion vector in the decoder will produce better outcomes in dialogue generation compared to other models.

An affect-rich neural conversational model with biased attention and weighted cross-entropy loss will produce responses that are appropriate in syntax and semantics, as well as rich in affect, outperforming state-of-the-art baseline models in generating natural and affect-rich responses.
Emotional dialogue management will increase user engagement in spoken conversational AI for video games.

The directed acyclic neural network, DAG-ERC, provides a more intuitive way to model the information flow between long-distance conversation background and nearby context for emotion recognition from conversation.

given_idea: Box embeddings provide improved performance on various word similarity tasks, particularly on less common words.

top_k.sentence: Embedding-based word similarities are highly sensitive to small changes in the training corpus for various algorithms.
Symmetric pattern based word embeddings will improve word similarity prediction compared to existing alternatives.
Bilingual word embeddings significantly out-perform baselines in word semantic similarity.
Embedding words as probability densities in a low-dimensional space will improve context-specific word representations compared to standard word embedding methods.
The proposed model outperforms the word-level embedding methods in both word similarity evaluation and word sense disambiguation.
The proposed method of estimating out-of-vocabulary word embeddings improves word similarity tasks and downstream tasks in Twitter and biomedical domains.
Various factors contribute to the stability of word embeddings.
The resulting embeddings capture semantic similarity better than regular distributional methods and other dictionary-based methods.
WordRank efficiently estimates word representations via robust ranking and outperforms state-of-the-art word embedding techniques on limited training sets.
Using word embeddings to predict the compositionality of multiword expressions will outperform count-based distributional similarity methods.
Neural word embedding methods implicitly factorize a word-context matrix, and using a sparse Shifted Positive PMI word-context matrix improves results on word similarity tasks.
Word embeddings capture both semantic and morphological similarity as affected by different types of linguistic properties.
The scalable Bayesian neural word embedding algorithm will demonstrate competitive performance for word analogy and similarity tasks compared to the original Skip-Gram method.
Personalized word embeddings yield improved performance in language modeling compared to generic word representations.
The proposed model will outperform existing word representations by a good margin on word similarity tasks across many datasets.
Neural word embeddings show robust, stable performance across larger-scale tasks compared to co-occurrence vectors.
N-ball embeddings, carrying category information, significantly outperform word embeddings in the test of nearest neighborhoods.
A robust model should transgress the initial notion of semantic similarity induced by word embeddings to learn a more human-like understanding of meaning.
Specializing semantic word embeddings for either similarity or relatedness leads to better capture of human intuitions and clear improvements in NLP tasks.
Complementary learning of word embeddings based on the continuous bag-of-words and skip-gram model will effectively improve the quality of initial embeddings.
Contextualized word representations perform better than static word embeddings on detecting meaning shift.
Contextualized word embeddings provide a model for word sense disambiguation and outperform existing methods on benchmark datasets.
Task-specific evaluations appear to be a better option for evaluating word embeddings compared to word analogies.
The method of reconstructing pre-trained word embeddings using subword information can effectively represent a large number of subword embeddings in a considerably small fixed space.
Leveraging both surface-form and context for learning embeddings of novel words results in large increases in embedding quality.
The novel learning method for word embeddings significantly outperforms a baseline based on a previously introduced word embedding method for semantic relation classification.
Combining Bilingual Word Embeddings with word surface similarity methods will improve performance on rare words in Bilingual Dictionary Induction.
Pretrained word embeddings helped either by reducing the training epochs or yielding significant performance gains.

The proposed approach will capture word sense relatedness in multi-prototype word embedding model and outperform both conventional single-prototype embedding models and other multi-prototype embedding models.
Dict2vec will improve word embeddings by using lexical dictionaries to create semantically-related word pairs.
Combining contextual word embeddings with permutation-based statistical tests will improve the detection of lexical semantic change in smaller data sets.
Corpus and query specific embeddings outperform globally trained word embeddings for retrieval tasks.
Vector differences of some embeddings reflect small changes within a sentence.

Replacing the embeddings of the important words in the input samples with the average of their synonyms’ embeddings can significantly improve the generalization of deep neural network-based classifiers.
ALONE can be used as word representation sufficiently through an experiment on the reconstruction of pre-trained word embeddings.
Probabilistic FastText outperforms both FastText and dictionary-level probabilistic embeddings on several word-similarity benchmarks.
The accuracy for different categories of linguistic relations is affected by window size and dimensionality of the SVD-based word embeddings.
The framework for intrinsic evaluation of word vector representations will reveal that state-of-the-art word embedding models have considerable room for improvement.
The quality of word embeddings depends on the input corpora, model architectures, and hyper-parameter settings.
The extension to the Skip-gram model efficiently learns multiple embeddings per word type, improving word sense discrimination and embedding learning.
Learning word embeddings from visual co-occurrences will yield better representations of visual concepts compared to text-only embeddings like GloVe.
The embedding-based features are very effective, and even a single embedding-based feature can beat the combination of several Bag-of-Words-based features.

Semantic change is linear in two senses: today's embedding vectors of words can be derived as linear combinations of embedding vectors of their neighbors in previous time periods, and self-similarity of words decays linearly in time.
Contextualized word embeddings capture human-like distinctions between English word senses, such as polysemy and homonymy.

Word Mover’s Embedding (WME) consistently matches or outperforms state-of-the-art techniques in text classification and textual similarity tasks, with significantly higher accuracy on problems of short length.
Removing bias from word embeddings will mitigate discrimination in various applications.
The geometric framework for learning meta-embeddings will effectively transform word embeddings into a common latent space, improving word similarity and analogy tasks.
Contextualised word embeddings will significantly improve lexical semantic change detection compared to existing methods.
Siamese Continuous Bag of Words model will provide high-quality sentence embeddings by optimizing word embeddings for sentence representation.

The EmbedAlign model embeds words in their complete observed context and learns by marginalisation of latent lexical alignments, achieving competitive results on several standard benchmarks including natural language inference, paraphrasing, and text similarity.

given_idea: Hyperparameter optimization methods often fail to outperform grid search due to insufficient time budget and overfitting.
original: NA

top_k.sentence: Random search for hyper-parameter optimization is more efficient than grid search and manual search, finding better models within a smaller fraction of computation time.
The proposed hyperparameter tuning algorithm finds good hyperparameter settings nearly an order of magnitude faster than random search.
The proposed hyperparameter optimization method consistently outperforms both Bayesian optimization and Hyperband on a wide range of problem types.
An algorithm for inexpensive gradient-based hyperparameter optimization will jointly tune weights and hyperparameters with only a few times more cost in memory and compute than standard training.
Bayesian optimization is superior to random search for machine learning hyperparameter tuning.

Maximum a Posteriori Policy Optimisation (MPO) outperforms existing methods with respect to sample efficiency, premature convergence and robustness to hyperparameter settings while achieving similar or better final performance.
Determining good values for hyperparameters is complex and requires a disciplined, theoretically sound search strategy.
Efficient methods can provide insight into the relationship between hyperparameter settings and performance in machine learning.
The forward-mode procedure is suitable for real-time hyperparameter updates, which may significantly speed up hyperparameter optimization on large datasets.

The methodology to optimize hyperparameters based on the computation of the gradient of a model selection criterion will improve the efficiency of hyperparameter selection in machine learning algorithms.
HyperPower enables efficient Bayesian optimization and random search in the context of power- and memory-constrained hyperparameter optimization for neural networks.
Efficient ways to set hyper-parameters will significantly reduce training time and improve performance in deep learning.
Hyperband can provide over an order-of-magnitude speedup over popular Bayesian optimization methods on a variety of deep-learning and kernel-based learning problems.

The proposed approach speeds up state-of-the-art hyperparameter optimization methods for deep neural networks roughly twofold, enabling them to find deep neural network settings that yield better performance than those chosen by human experts.
Population Based Training will discover a schedule of hyperparameter settings that leads to faster convergence and higher final performance in neural networks.
Algorithmic approaches can find better results for hyper-parameter optimization in training neural networks and deep belief networks.
Large-scale learning problems are subject to a qualitatively different tradeoff involving the computational complexity of the underlying optimization algorithms.
Overparametrization in neural networks simplifies the optimization landscape and allows for good prediction error on unseen data despite interpolating the training set.
Shampoo converges considerably faster than commonly used optimizers in stochastic optimization over tensor spaces.
A meta-modeling approach to support automated hyperparameter optimization will provide practical tools that replace hand-tuning with a reproducible and unbiased optimization process.
Optimizer performance varies greatly across tasks.
AutoNE will significantly outperform existing methods in optimizing hyperparameters for network embedding on massive networks by requiring less time and fewer trials.
The choice of function Q associated with the simulated annealing algorithm must be as large as possible to provide a fast rate of convergence.
Batched Gaussian Process Bandit Optimization via Determinantal Point Processes will outperform state-of-the-art methods in hyper-parameter optimization tasks.
Recent advances in neural networks could very well be attributed to mundane issues like better hyperparameter optimization.
The validity of an algorithm whose running time goes up slightly faster than the square of d is proven.
The novel adaptive optimization method will allow for larger models and mini-batches while maintaining per-parameter adaptivity and sublinear memory cost.
Simple heuristics such as epsilon-greedy and Boltzmann exploration outperform theoretically sound algorithms on most settings by a significant margin.

Most generative adversarial network models can reach similar scores with enough hyperparameter optimization and random restarts, suggesting that improvements can arise from a higher computational budget and tuning more than fundamental algorithmic changes.
The proposed algorithm achieves the highest possible rate of convergence for classical optimization and identification problems.
HYPERBAND can provide over an order of magnitude speedups over competitors on a variety of neural network and kernel-based learning problems.

Second-order stochastic methods for optimization problems in machine learning will match the per-iteration cost of gradient-based methods and improve upon the overall running time over popular first-order methods.

The combination of momentum-based gradient descent optimizers and scale-invariant parameters leads to the premature decay of effective step sizes and sub-optimal model performances.
The expected convergence rate of without-replacement sampling is faster than that of with-replacement sampling in least means squares optimization.

Using a fixed dropout probability during training is a suboptimal choice, and an adaptive regularization scheme that smoothly increases the difficulty of the optimization problem leads to better generalization.

Parallel and distributed implementation of Thompson sampling (PDTS) outperforms other scalable baselines such as a greedy search, epsilon-greedy approaches, and a random search method in large-scale parallel Bayesian optimization.
TandA generates more stable and robust models reducing the effort required for selecting optimal hyper-parameters.
Bayesian optimization solves problems by a progressive and adaptive approach via updating surrogate model from a small piece of data at each time.
Increasing depth in deep networks can speed up optimization by acting as a preconditioner, particularly in overparameterized settings.
The algorithms require space that is only polynomial in n and d, while achieving query times that are sub-linear in n and polynomial in d.

Continuous Sparsification, a new algorithm to search for winning tickets, consistently finds sparse sub-networks which train faster and better than the overparameterized models they were extracted from.
The pre-computational complexity model provides significant speedups compared to the value oracle model for large scale submodular optimization problems.
Adaptive scaling can handle the positive sparsity problem and directly optimize over F-measure via dynamic cost-sensitive learning.
Predictive Entropy Search will obtain approximations that are more accurate and efficient than Entropy Search for global optimization of black-box functions.
LOKI can learn to outperform a suboptimal expert and converge faster than running policy gradient from scratch.
Comparing total-order estimators in an eight-dimension hypercube significantly reduces the dependency of results on benchmark design.
The model structure used with well-tuned hyper-parameters leads to a significant improvement compared to the previous state-of-the-art system.

The Bayesian approach empirically outperforms both the existing frequentist counterpart and other Bayesian optimization methods in finding the maximizer of a nonlinear function under budget constraints.
Fabolas finds high-quality solutions 10 to 100 times faster than other state-of-the-art Bayesian optimization methods.

given_idea: CoRRPUS system and abstracted prompting procedures can beat current state-of-the-art structured LLM techniques on pre-existing story understanding tasks with minimal hand engineering.

top_k.sentence: The architecture that augments story processing with an external dynamic knowledge graph will facilitate better story comprehension than prompts composed only of story text.
StoryBuddy is an AI-enabled system that accommodates dynamic user needs for parent involvement and child engagement in interactive storytelling.
The character-based interactive storytelling prototype will support story generation and any-time user intervention using hierarchical task network planning techniques.
The models learn narrative chains, script-like structures that we evaluate with the narrative cloze task.
The framework achieves controllable story generation with lower perplexity and enhanced coherence through the introduction of storylines.

Increased types of human collaboration at both planning and writing stages results in a 10-50% improvement in story quality as compared to less interactive baselines.

Incorporating structured knowledge into the story generation process enhances the logical coherence among story events and alleviates over-generalization and repetition problems in stories.

Hierarchical story generation will significantly improve the relevance of generated stories to prompts compared to non-hierarchical models.

KgPLM will contain richer factual knowledge than conventional pre-trained language models and will achieve state-of-the-art performance on machine reading comprehension tasks.

Current natural language processing technology can be used to automatically produce plot unit representations for narrative text.

The ScriptWriter model significantly outperforms the baselines that simply use the narrative as a kind of context.
FewVLM with prompt-based learning outperforms Frozen which is 31x larger than FewVLM by 18.2% point and achieves comparable results to a 246x larger model, PICa.

The programmatic large language model prompt structure enables plan generation functional across situated environments, robot capabilities, and tasks.
A typology that captures the main similarities and differences among assessment tasks will aid in the design of new tasks and address challenges in narrative reading comprehension.
